publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 169-173. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: I am very glad of yr letter,-- & perhaps a little glad that you "miss me"!--
It shows at least that I have not become irksome or odious to you. If you will have patience yet a little longer, you will rejoice that you had patience.--
Now as to my health: I do not agree with you that

the progress is slow. When we consider that I have not been quite 2 months under treatment, for a most serious complaint which had existed (more or less) for upwards of a year,-- I think we cannot call the present state other than very satisfactory. Not only is there an improvement in nerves & in general health which is obvious to everyone, & is most of all

felt & known to myself,-- but the local condition is no longer vicious . Dr Locock explained to me yesterday how threatening & how morbid it had been.-- He said that tho' now there is still an extensive & deep-seated sore, yet it is a healthy sore that is not pressing hourly on the vital system. When he first saw me, he says he feared almost lest the constitution might not
have power to aid the remedies & to restore parts that were so un-vitalized as it were. Now he says he has no shadow of doubt or anxiety.

He is shortly going away, & will have to leave me under Dr Cape's care,-- & this is one reason why my peculiar attention from him (Dr L--) is right at this time, in order that he may leave Dr Cape in full possession of all that is desirable
2/ during the 5 or 6 weeks of Dr. L's absence. This transfer to Dr Cape is to me extremely distressing & unpleasant, but it is inevitable.--

I may tell you that there is a possibility of my going down on Satdy, till Mondy. But as it is so entirely uncertain, I have not liked even to mention it,-- & you had better not think of it as likely.
One comfort is that the drive to & fro is rather good for me. And I think no harm for the horses either, with discretion.

As to Nelson: will you explain to Kayes that 1stly, the nature of the wound did not seem to admit of the solution of a bite. It had not the character of a bite. 2ndly, that last year in the autumn (It might have

been Novr or Decr) when the same thing occured, the accident was seen , for the Coachman was looking at the dog at the time, & heard him yell at the momeent he saw the stone hit his foot. The stone was then thrown either from the garden or [two words crossed out] from some of the side nooks & covers & alleys that may border the road to the Tunnel. No other dog, nor any
(visible) person was near at the time.
I do not believe that any dog was near Nelson this time either. A fight & quarrel would have ensued & been heard, & there is no doubt it would have been notorious enough.
I will try & get the Coachman to say on whom his suspicions fall. Meanwhile please to state the above facts. I examined the wound both times, &

3/ it certainly was very different from any bite I ever saw, & was not on a spot where the teeth of an antagonist could well have found purchase. There was a cut & bruise laying the claw bare, on the broad part of the foot. A dog would bite higher.
Is the enclosed the Nicholson marriage?--
